title: Protecting and projecting national interest
sub_topics: Chinese schoolbook teaching on SCS ownership
Hong Kong and Macau under long-term leases
Korea / Vietnam / Afghanistan / Iraq US interventions
Marcos Jr. reversion to US alliance

stances.claim: The unsaid short and simple answer to why China and the US behave inconsistently with their formal legal positions on UNCLOS and the Arbitral Award is 'national interest' — all states act to protect and project what benefits them, and national interest is not constant; it shifts with present and future aspirations.

Any Chinese leader who refuses or neglects to protect the decades-long teaching that the SCS belongs to China would be deemed a traitor or a nincompoop to be ousted summarily — Chinese aggression is structurally compelled by domestic political pedagogy.
stances.rhetorical_move: political-incentive analysis grounding aggression in domestic constraints

stances.claim: The US too has changed its aspirations — from pre-1941 neutrality to Pax Americana spreading American-style democracy, libertarianism, capitalism, and free trade; and now Trump 'loves being a disruptor' by demanding allies share peacekeeping costs.
stances.rhetorical_move: historical-arc narrative of American national interest as evolving construct

stances.claim: Philippine national interest 'see-sawed from America to China and back to America' — Duterte's 2016 landslide on autocratic appeal, then Marcos Jr.'s overwhelming 2022 absolute majority (over 50% — first under the 1987 Constitution) on reversion to the US alliance.
stances.rhetorical_move: comparative-electoral mandate framing for foreign-policy shifts

stances.claim: With his overwhelming mandate, President Marcos Jr. can lead the protection and projection of Philippine national interest vis-à-vis ASEAN, China, the US, and the world.

cases: Republic of the Philippines v. People's Republic of China (2016 Arbitral Award)
laws_treaties: United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS)
events: 2022 Philippine presidential election (Marcos Jr. absolute majority)

signature_phrases: Verily, he loves being a disruptor
the see-sawing shifts in our national interest
register_markers: I asked rhetorically in last Monday's column
THE UNSAID SHORT AND SIMPLE ANSWER
THE UNITED STATES, TOO, HAS CHANGED
IN 2016, THE FILIPINOS, TOO, GOT TIRED
decision_framework_signals: applies national-interest lens (Palmerston realism) to all states' conduct including the Philippines'
frames Chinese aggression as structurally compelled by domestic political pedagogy rather than mere expansionism
uses historical-arc framing (1939-1945 → Pax Americana → Trump disruption) for American national interest evolution
uses electoral-mandate framing to validate foreign-policy shifts in democratic states
cross_references: last Monday's column ('The Maritime Zones Act, Asean, and China')
